critique.summary: The article discusses BYD's new Flash Chargers capable of charging some EV batteries from 10 to 70 percent in five minutes by delivering up to 1,500 kW, significantly faster than typical 350 kW chargers. Despite this advancement, experts note that such ultra-fast charging may not dramatically change daily EV usage due to infrastructure limitations and the prevalence of home charging.
mainPoints: BYD's Flash Chargers can charge EV batteries from 10 to 70 percent in five minutes using up to 1,500 kW power.
BYD integrates its own cars, batteries, and chargers, enabling faster charging speeds, exemplified by the Denza Z9GT with the new Blade battery.
The new Blade battery uses lithium manganese iron phosphate (LMFP) chemistry, improving energy density by about 5 percent over previous versions.
Only BYD's own vehicles currently support these ultra-fast charging speeds, limiting broader applicability.
Infrastructure and grid capacity pose significant challenges to deploying such high-power chargers widely, especially in the US.
Most US EV owners rely on home charging, making ultra-fast public charging less impactful for daily use.
The US EV market faces challenges including automaker cancellations, policy setbacks, and slower charging infrastructure development compared to China and Europe.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about BYD's charging technology and battery chemistry align with publicly available information about BYD's advancements and the technical challenges of ultra-fast EV charging. The reported charging speeds and power levels are consistent with BYD's announcements and industry analyses. Expert opinions cited, such as from UC Davis, are credible and reflect common understanding in the EV field. The article accurately highlights infrastructure and grid limitations as real barriers to widespread adoption of such fast chargers. The mention of US market challenges and automaker decisions is also supported by recent news. Overall, the article is factually accurate and balanced.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore the potential impacts of such fast charging on battery longevity and degradation.
It does not address the cost implications for consumers or operators of installing and maintaining 1,500 kW charging stations.
The environmental impact of increased electricity demand and the source of that electricity are not discussed.
Potential regulatory or safety challenges related to ultra-high-power charging infrastructure are not mentioned.
The article focuses mainly on BYD and does not compare similar developments by other manufacturers beyond Tesla.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the feasibility and impact of ultra-fast EV charging technology like BYD's, with users debating infrastructure limitations, battery longevity, and practical benefits for long-distance travel. Some express skepticism about the technology's real-world applicability and political or economic barriers, while others share personal experiences highlighting the need for faster charging.

comments.author: RevEng
comments.text:
I wonder if Gil Tal has ever used an EV as their daily vehicle.

I have had two EVs in the last three years - a Kona and an IONIQ 5. I have greatly enjoyed them both. But one thing was a downside that I just had to accept: poor charging.

Granted, I live in the Canadian Prairies full of small towns a fair distance apart. And it's not exactly progressive - I'm actually being taxed for owning an EV. The charging infrastructure is sparse with 50-100kW charges every 100km. On long distance trips I spend 1 hour charging for every 2 hours driving. To say that faster charging wouldn't make a meaningful difference is simply wrong. Sure, it doesn't have to be 5 minutes - even 10-15 would be enough - but current chargers don't get anywhere close to that, even with 350kW, which rarely if ever reach those charging speeds.

For driving around the city I never bat an eye. I have a level 2 charger in my garage and there's one at work that is decently priced should I ever need it. I never use a fast charger for local travel. But long distance travel is what people are worried about and having much faster charging would most certainly make a difference for me and for them.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed personal experience that supports the argument that faster charging is beneficial for long-distance EV travel, making it a credible and constructive contribution.

comments.author: nneonneo
comments.text:
Based on the figures here, they’re claiming around 400 miles of range added in 300 seconds (60% of the full 677 mile range); contrast this with around 100 seconds for a typical gas pump (8 gal/min) and typical efficiency (30 mpg). It suggests that you’d need around 5MW chargers to truly get to the speed of a gas pump.

On the other hand, 5 minutes is already a huge improvement over 15-30 minutes, and it’s fast enough to remove much of the friction of recharging an EV.

Really wish this kind of tech would come to North America…
comments.analysis: This comment uses quantitative reasoning to compare charging speeds to gas pumps, offering a nuanced perspective that acknowledges improvements while noting practical limits.

comments.author: Tade0
comments.text:
I have a feeling that half the reason they're doing this is that they don't have a good idea how to increase energy efficiency.

Case in point:

2026 BMW i3 - 900km WLTP from a 108kWh battery.

2026 Denza Z9 GT - 800km WLTP from a 122kWh pack.

The former charges at a maximum of 400kW, while the latter at over twice that which saves... about 10 minutes at the charger after 450km of driving(12 vs 22 minutes approx).

Many such examples with Chinese manufacturers putting 700kg battery packs into the vehicles just to be able to say it's this and that kWh.

I don't know about anyone here but after 400km or so I'm done and want to at least stretch my legs.
comments.analysis: This comment critically examines the marginal time savings of faster charging relative to battery size and driving habits, providing a realistic assessment of the technology's practical benefits.

comments.author: ekr
comments.text:
Although the thought of getting an electric car has passed through my mind on a few occasions, I'm not 100% familiar with the intricate technical details. (for some reason, the tax incentives where I live are still in favor of continuing with the small petrol car I have. Taxes are primarily a function of weight in the Netherlands, and anything besides a lightweight Dacia Spring would imply significantly higher monthly expenditure for me).

What I'm wondering w.r.t. this article is: wouldn't such fast charging shorten the battery lifespan?

I have experience with ebike batteries. Bosch in particular, with very decent 29E samsung cells, that after 70k km or so, basically halved their capacity. I imagine this effect is severily reduced with a car battery because there are a lot more than 10p, so all the wear is distributed more evenly, and 29E are very old technology.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about battery degradation from fast charging, supported by related experience, though it acknowledges differences between ebike and car batteries.

comments.author: orbital-decay
comments.text:
>Just taking an existing fast charger with 150- or 350-kW capacity and swapping in the latest and greatest 1,500-kW chargers wouldn’t get anyone faster speeds. The system would need all new “pipes”—grid capacity—to actually move that much current.

The grid doesn't necessarily mean "pipes" or power lines. You don't build a pipeline to every gas station. Mobile charging robots work pretty well in China.
comments.analysis: Offers an alternative perspective on infrastructure challenges, suggesting innovative solutions like mobile charging robots, which is a constructive and plausible point.

comments.author: christkv
comments.text: Absolute garbage. Just stop and think for one second what kind of power delivery is required to do this and you will quickly realize that’s it’s not feasible anywhere other than as a demo.
comments.analysis: This dismissive comment lacks supporting evidence and is overly emotional, reducing its credibility despite raising a valid concern about power delivery challenges.

comments.author: mbfg
comments.text: More importantly, the US has banned these cars in America to give protection to american manufacturers.
comments.analysis: This claim is unsubstantiated and appears to be a conspiracy theory, contributing little to the technical discussion.

comments.author: glimshe
comments.text: Cool! My only concern is that Wired has a very long and consistent history of advertising technologies that don't work quite as they say. So let's hope this is real.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about the source's reliability, which is a reasonable caution but does not provide technical insight.

comments.author: soared
comments.text: Is this how the US falls behind? Missing technological improvements due to blind disagreements with Chinese/etc, combined with inability to update infrastructure? (Unclear how/why but datacenters being stood up so quickly seems like an exception to US’s bad construction)
comments.analysis: Raises a broader geopolitical and infrastructural concern, which is relevant but somewhat speculative without detailed evidence.
